target: SEC-6.6 (The Accuracy Claim) — restructure for lead & CERN preemption
finding: SEC-6.6 restructure: lead with internal-data incoherence (not manual HTML), acknowledge manual-entry defense, add CERN-preemption paragraph grounding the denominator-exclusion argument in pre-registration vs post-hoc reclassification.
summary: Two linked moderate issues on SEC-6.6. ISS-1033: the current lead paragraph argues from 'manually entered HTML,' which a dome defender can defuse by pointing out that academic journals also hardcode final statistics — manual entry alone is not damning. The stronger lead is that the dome's own internal data sources produce at least four different accuracy figures (api/scorecard 96.3%, api/current/results 100.0%, wins-page with open problems 89.3%, pre-V51.1 denominator 94.7%), none matching the 94.5% headline. Incoherence is the bust; manual entry is a corroborating symptom. ISS-1034: the denominator-exclusion paragraph does not preempt the obvious defense that exclusions are standard practice (CERN excludes preliminary runs; trials exclude pre-enrollment data). The distinguishing feature of principled exclusion is pre-registration. The dome's excluded items were initially tracked as active predictions — W001/W004 in a tracked denominator, W019/W020/W024/W025/W027 as active WINs — and were reclassified only after failure. This is post-hoc reclassification after unblinding, analogous to protocol deviation. This expansion restructures the four prose paragraphs in SEC-6.6 accordingly and updates the TLDR to lead with incoherence.

kernel_acknowledged.iss_1033_kernel: Manual entry of final statistics is routine in published work — academic papers and journal PDFs hardcode their own tables in the final typeset version. A 'hardcoded HTML' argument by itself does not prove fraud or error; it proves only that the figure did not update automatically. The current lead paragraph reads as if manual entry were the scandal, and a sophisticated defender (or the dome author himself) can defuse it in one sentence.
kernel_acknowledged.iss_1034_kernel: Principled exclusions are a real and correct part of scientific methodology. CERN does exclude preliminary or low-luminosity runs from headline Higgs-channel results; medical trials exclude pre-enrollment data and patients who fail screening; forecasting scorecards exclude entries below a confidence threshold. A dome defender invoking any of these is not lying — they are invoking legitimate practice. The critique is not against exclusion per se, it is against exclusion-after-unblinding: the dome's excluded items were not pre-specified criteria applied to incoming data, they were previously-active predictions retroactively recategorized after the data came in wrong.

root_causes.iss_1033: SEC-6.6 P1 leads with 'The dome's homepage has headlined accuracy figures that change manually between versions' and spends its first two paragraphs on source-code archaeology (which files don't compute the figure). This rhetorical order assumes the reader already accepts that manual entry of a summary statistic is itself suspect. Dome defenders don't accept that premise, and they don't have to — it is a weak premise. The disagreement across internal endpoints is a much stronger lead because it doesn't depend on any assumption about how summary statistics should be produced; it just shows the model contradicting itself.
root_causes.iss_1034: SEC-6.6 P3 asserts 'The denominator is an editorial choice' and enumerates the excluded items but never addresses the obvious legitimate-exclusion defense. The argument as written is therefore incomplete: it documents the exclusion without distinguishing it from principled exclusions. A preemption paragraph is required — not because the defense is compelling, but because it's the first thing a competent defender will say, and SEC-6.6 loses a round against a competent defender every time.

accuracy_variants.headline: 94.5% (V51.1), 95.2% (V51.0)
accuracy_variants.observation: No two internal sources agree on accuracy. The headline matches none of them. The poller already re-checks these endpoints; the accuracy_variants block in uncounted-failures.json is already the system of record.

reclassified_items.source: dome tracking page + V50.6 → V51.x history
reclassified_items.pre_registration_argument: To invoke the CERN-style defense legitimately, the dome would need documented evidence that W001, W004, W019, W020, W024, W025, and W027 were flagged as 'low confidence' or 'preliminary' before their outcomes were known. They were not. Each appeared in the dome's active predictions registry with a WIN number and contributed to the denominator, and was demoted only after the expected result failed to arrive. The git-versioned prediction page in monitor.py captures these demotions as diffs.

section_6_6_patch.target_file: data/sections.json → part6.html → details block with id "p6-66-5" (section 6.6)
section_6_6_patch.new_tldr_html: The accuracy claim is a tails-I-win, heads-you-lose system. The dome's own internal data sources produce at least four different accuracy figures — none matching the headline — which makes the figure an editorial choice rather than a measurement. The numerator is pumped by counting non-discriminating predictions as "confirmed," the denominator is kept small by excluding failed predictions that were originally tracked as active, and the headline itself is a hand-edited HTML string with no script in the repository that derives it.

new_prose_paragraphs_in_order.html: The headline figure doesn't match the dome's own internal data. Querying the dome repository's scoring and results endpoints returns {{ACCURACY_VARIANT_LIST}} — different figures depending on which data source and counting method is used. In detail: {{ACCURACY_VARIANT_DETAIL}}. The V51.1 headline is 94.5% (69/73). No two of these agree, and the headline is not a rounding artifact of any of them — the wins-page arithmetic including open problems drops to 89.3%, the pre-V51.1 denominator gives 94.7%, the scorecard API gives 96.3%, and the current-results API gives 100.0%. An accuracy claim that cannot be reconciled with the model's own tracking data is an editorial summary, not a computed metric. The other signs below (manually edited headline, post-hoc denominator, non-independent predictions) corroborate that pattern; the disagreement itself is the primary evidence.

The headline is a hand-edited string, not a computed quantity. A defender might reply that academic papers and journals also hardcode their own statistics once a manuscript is finalized — and that's fair; manual entry alone is not damning. The question isn't whether the string is typed by hand, it's whether the string agrees with the data it claims to summarize. Here it doesn't (see paragraph above). The repository source corroborates the manual entry: in V51.1 the accuracy figure appears as <div class="score-number score-green">94.5%</div> in the homepage markup, and no script anywhere in the repository computes an overall accuracy percentage — we checked scoring.js (per-prediction point-based scoring, no aggregate), predictions.js, build.js, analytics.js, apply_scoring_schema.py, recalc_v51.py, compile_exhaustive_api.py, verify_predictions.py, and build_tracking.py. The V51.0-to-V51.1 update (95.2% → 94.5%) required a hand edit rather than a recount. The V51.0 HTML even displayed the arithmetic 67 / (67 + 4) = 94.4% while labeling it "95.2%" — a figure that didn't match its own displayed formula. A genuinely computed metric would update automatically and wouldn't disagree with its own arithmetic; this one does both.

The denominator is an editorial choice. The V51.0 formula {{TOTAL_WINS}} / ({{TOTAL_WINS}} + 4) = 95.2% treated {{TOTAL_WINS}} WINs as confirmed and excluded 4 as "below detection threshold" (W001, W004). V51.1 added 2 new WINs and updated the headline to 94.5% (69/73) — but the denominator logic is identical, and the figure is still manually entered. The model's own tracking page lists 4 predictions as failed — 1 Falsified (W024) and 3 labeled "Refined" (W019, W020, W027), and WIN-025 was removed entirely. Including these: {{TOTAL_WINS}} / {{HONEST_ACCURACY_DENOM}} ≈ {{HONEST_ACCURACY}}.

A defender might reply that exclusions are standard practice — CERN excludes preliminary runs from published Higgs-channel results, biomedical trials exclude pre-enrollment data, and reputable prediction scorecards exclude low-confidence entries. Correct: principled exclusions are standard. The distinguishing feature is when the exclusion criterion is committed. CERN pre-registers its blinding and selection cuts before unblinding the data. Biomedical trials fix enrollment criteria and primary endpoints before randomization. The dome's excluded items were not pre-registered. Each was initially tracked as an active prediction — assigned a WIN number, listed on the predictions page, included in the denominator — and was reclassified as "Refined," "below detection threshold," or removed only after the expected result failed to materialize. Reclassifying a test after you've seen the answer is a protocol deviation after unblinding, not an exclusion criterion. Standard scientific practice allows the former, not the latter; the dome's denominator is built entirely from the latter.

More fundamentally, accuracy metrics are meaningful only when the predictions are independent and discriminating. As our point-by-point review shows, many WINs are re-sliced versions of the same data (WIN-040 through 043 are all SAA positioning from the same CHAOS-7 dataset), known constants (tidal periods), or globe values relabeled (Hubble Law, WGS84 gravity). Counting each as an independent "confirmed prediction" inflates both the numerator and the headline. Combined with the disagreement across the dome's own internal sources (above), the hand-edited headline string, and the post-hoc denominator exclusions, the "94.5% accuracy" figure is best understood as an editorial claim about the model's favorable framing rather than a computed measurement of predictive success.

section_6_6_patch.notes_for_decider: The new block is four prose paragraphs plus one TLDR, preserving the existing <details id="p6-66-5"> wrapper and summary/ps-detail structure. All template placeholders used ({{ACCURACY_VARIANT_LIST}}, {{ACCURACY_VARIANT_DETAIL}}, {{TOTAL_WINS}}, {{HONEST_ACCURACY_DENOM}}, {{HONEST_ACCURACY}}) are already resolved by build-scripts/generate-html.js (see lines 1085-1132). No build-script changes required. Recommend running `node build.js html` after integration to verify the variants render cleanly; the accuracy_variants in uncounted-failures.json are already the canonical data.

defender_counter_attack_surface.strongest_remaining_defense_post_patch: 'The four internal variants count different things — scorecard tracks sub-predictions, current-results tracks test outcomes, wins page tracks WINs — so it's not surprising they disagree; the headline is specifically the WINs metric.'
defender_counter_attack_surface.rebuttal_ready: (a) Fair point in principle, but the variants are not clearly labeled as counting different units anywhere on the dome site; the visitor sees '94.5% accuracy' without disambiguation, and the repository does not publish a reconciliation table. (b) Even restricting to the WINs metric, the pre-V51.1 arithmetic (67/71 = 94.7%) disagrees with the V51.1 headline (94.5%), and the wins-page-with-open-problems arithmetic (67/75 = 89.3%) also disagrees — both of these count 'WINs,' just with different denominator choices. The inconsistency survives within the same metric family. (c) The onus is on the dome to publish which internal source is canonical and why the others are wrong; absent that, each endpoint is a self-assertion of accuracy and the headline is one choice among several.
defender_counter_attack_surface.secondary_defense: 'CERN's pre-registration standards are overkill for a hobby model; this is Popperian prediction-tracking, not a regulated clinical trial.'
defender_counter_attack_surface.secondary_rebuttal: The dome author explicitly invokes scientific-prediction language ('confirmed predictions,' 'falsified,' 'tracked against observed data'). Whichever standard is invoked sets the methodology: if the claim is informal, it cannot also claim 94.5% accuracy as a falsification-tested metric; if the claim is formal, it must meet the pre-registration standard the formal frame implies.

cross_references_for_decider.where: SEC-6.6 TLDR (ps-tldr inside the summary for p6-66-5)
cross_references_for_decider.action: Replace with the tldr in section_6_6_patch.new_tldr_html

cross_references_for_decider.where: SEC-6.6 body P1-P4 (inside ps-detail div for p6-66-5)
cross_references_for_decider.action:
Replace the four existing

paragraphs with the four new paragraphs in section_6_6_patch.new_prose_paragraphs_in_order (order matters — P1 lead on incoherence, P2 manual-entry-as-corroboration, P3 denominator + CERN preemption, P4 independence)
No change required — all template placeholders used are already resolved (see context object lines 1133-1155).
No change required — dome_accuracy_variants block already provides the canonical list.

tldr_evidence: The dome repository exposes at least four internal accuracy figures (scorecard 96.3%, current-results 100.0%, wins-page-with-open-problems 89.3%, pre-V51.1 denominator 94.7%) that all disagree with the headline 94.5%. The headline is also a hand-edited HTML string with no script in the repository that computes it, and the denominator excludes seven items (W001, W004, W019, W020, W024, W025, W027) that were originally tracked as active predictions and reclassified only after they failed.
tldr_verdict: SEC-6.6 argument restructured to lead with the strongest point (internal incoherence) and to preempt the legitimate-exclusion defense (pre-registration vs post-hoc reclassification is the line), rather than relying on the 'manually entered HTML' point which a sophisticated defender can defuse in one sentence.
next_steps_for_decider: Apply the SEC-6.6 TLDR replacement (section_6_6_patch.new_tldr_html) inside the <summary> for details id p6-66-5.
Apply the four-paragraph replacement (section_6_6_patch.new_prose_paragraphs_in_order) inside the <div class="ps-detail"> for the same block, preserving paragraph order.

Run `node build.js html` after integration to verify that {{ACCURACY_VARIANT_LIST}}, {{ACCURACY_VARIANT_DETAIL}}, {{TOTAL_WINS}}, {{HONEST_ACCURACY_DENOM}}, and {{HONEST_ACCURACY}} all resolve correctly (all five are already handled by generate-html.js).
Close ISS-1033 and ISS-1034 simultaneously; both are satisfied by this single restructure.
Optional: add a small test in test.js that asserts SEC-6.6 contains the phrase 'pre-register' or 'post-hoc' so regressions that strip the CERN preemption are caught.

follow_up_issues_suggested.title: SEC-6.6: consider publishing the accuracy_variants table as a visible element on the page, not just embedded in prose
follow_up_issues_suggested.reason: A compact side-by-side table (endpoint → formula → result → notes) would make the 'none match the headline' point visually obvious to skimming readers and would resist future attempts by the dome to rewrite any single endpoint.

follow_up_issues_suggested.title: Poller: confirm the four accuracy_variants endpoints are re-fetched at every poll and that changes are flagged
follow_up_issues_suggested.reason: The api/current/results.json variant already rose from 33/34 (97.0%) to 35/35 (100.0%) on 2026-04-09. Future drift will either expose further incoherence or, if the dome quietly reconciles the endpoints with the headline, will itself be newsworthy as a reactive patch.

newsworthy: Dome accuracy headline contradicts the dome's own repository — scorecard says 96.3%, results API says 100%, wins-page arithmetic says 89.3%, and the hand-edited banner says 94.5%. No two agree.
